content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

February 4, 1992


Brien J. Farrell
Assistant City Attorney
City of Santa Rosa
100 Santa Rosa Avenue
P.O. Box 1678
Santa Rosa, CA  95402-1678

                                                Re:  Your Request for Advice
                                                     Our File No. I-92-063

Dear Mr. Farrell:

     This is in response to your letter requesting advice on
behalf of Bonnie Band who is an investment analyst with the City
of Santa Rosa.  You have requested advice concerning Ms. Band's
responsibilities under the conflict-of-interest provisions of the
Political Reform Act.¹  Because your request does not involve a
pending governmental decision, we are treating your request as one
for informal assistance.²


                                     QUESTION

     As an investment analyst with the City of Santa Rosa, what
restrictions does the Act impose on Ms. Band were she to accept
employment with Bank of America which has numerous dealings with
the city?


_____________________________
¹ Government Code Sections 81000-91015.  All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.  Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq.  All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)


428 J Street, Suite 800  •  P.O. Box 807  •  Sacramento CA 95804-0807  •  (916)322-5660

Our File No. I-92-063
Page 2


CONCLUSION

As a public official employed by the City of Santa Rosa,
Ms. Band will be prohibited from making, participating in making
or in any way attempting to use her official position to influence
a governmental decision which will have a foreseeable and material
financial effect, distinguishable from the effect on the public
generally, on any source of income of $250 or more within 12
months prior to the decision.

FACTS

Ms. Band is an investment analyst for the City of Santa Rosa.
You stated that as an investment analyst Ms. Band is responsible
for the day-to-day investment of the city's inactive reserves, the
monitoring of city accounts and the resolution of routine problems
with the city's bank, Bank of America. Ms. Band's duties also
include monitoring the availability of funds and negotiating
securities for the city. While the ultimate responsibility for
such decisions is held by the city treasurer, and the decisions
are reviewed by the city's financial analyst and by an investment
management committee, Ms. Band has the primary responsibility for
treasury management.

You stated that Ms. Band purchases securities through four
primary securities dealers through a competitive bid process, one
of which is the Bank of America. As of December 31, 1991, less
than 20% of the city's investments were purchased through the Bank
of America.

Ms. Band's position is not one currently designated in the
city's conflict of interest code. However, you stated that she
does participate in decisions which could financially affect her
interests. Furthermore, Ms. Band serves on a committee created to
evaluate proposals to solicit bids for a new banking contract.
This committee will also evaluate the bids, if bids are taken, and
make recommendations to the city.

Ms. Band has recently been offered a part-time position at
the Bank of America. You are concerned about potential conflicts
of interest that may result.

ANALYSIS

Public Officials

Section 32043 defines "public official" as every member,
officer, employee or consultant of a state or local government
agency. As an employee of the City of Santa Rosa, Ms. Band is a
public official subject to the conflict of interest provisions of
the Act.

Our File No. I-92-063
Page 3


Economic Interests

Section 87100 of the Act prohibits a public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise using her official position to influence a governmental decision in which she has a financial interest.3 You stated in our telephone conversation and in your letter that as an investment analyst for the City of Santa Rosa, Ms. Band is responsible for the day-to-day investment of city funds. You also stated that her position was more than purely clerical or ministerial and that her position entailed the making of decisions.

Section 87103 specifies that a public official has a financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from the effect on the public generally, on the official or a member of his or her immediate family or on:

(c) Any new source of income, other than gifts and other than loans by a commercial lending institution in the regular course of business on terms available to the public without regard to official status, aggregating two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value provided to, received by or promised to the public official within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is made.

In your letter you stated that Ms. Band wished to accept a salaried position with Bank of America. Any person or business entity, including Bank of America, that makes a payment to Ms. Band is a source of income to her for the purposes of Section 87103. Consequently, if Bank of America has been a source of income to Ms. Band of $250 or more within the 12 months prior to any decision which will reasonably foreseeably and materially financially affect Bank of America, Ms. Band may not participate


3 The Act also requires that every public official, including designated employees, disclose all their economic interests that could foreseeably be affected by the exercise of the official's duties. (Sections 81002(c), 87200-87313.) Section 82019 defines those employees who must be designated as any employees whose position entails the making or participation in the making of decisions which may foreseeably have a material effect on any financial interest. Since you stated that Ms. Band makes or participates in making decisions which could foreseeably have a material financial effect on her financial interest, she should be designated in the city's conflict of interest code and is subject to the disclosure provisions of the Act.

Our File No. I-92-063
Page 4


in the decision.⁴

Foreseeability and Materiality

Whether the financial consequences of a decision are
reasonably foreseeable at the time a governmental decision is made
depends on the facts of each particular case. An effect is
considered reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial
likelihood that it will occur. Certainty is not required.
However, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is not
reasonably foreseeable. (In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.)

However, in addition, the foreseeable effect on Ms. Band's
source of income must also be material to require
disqualification. The Commission has adopted differing guidelines
to determine whether an effect is material, depending on the
specific circumstances of each decision.

A. Interests Directly Involved in a Decision.

Where a source of income is directly before the city, as an
applicant or the subject of the decision, Regulation 18702.1(d)
provides that the effect of the decision on a source of income is
deemed material and disqualification is required. (ComEx Advice
Letter, No. A-89-177.) A source of income is directly before the
city when the source of income: (1) initiates the proceeding by
filing an application, claim, appeal, or similar request; (2) is a
named party to the proceeding; or (3) is the subject of a
proceeding because the decision involves the issuance, renewal,
approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other
entitlement to, or contract with, the subject person or business
entity. (Regulation 18702.1(b).)

Thus, where decisions involve contracts with the Bank of
America, investments in the bank, or new contracts on which the
bank has or will bid, the bank would be directly involved in the
decision and Ms. Band would not be able to participate.

B. Interests Indirectly Involved in a Decision.

Even if Bank of America is not directly involved in an agency
decision, Ms. Band will still be required to disqualify herself


⁴ Please note, if Ms. Band has a conflict of interest as an
investment advisor, she will also have the same conflict of
interest with respect to the decisions of other governmental
entities, such as the banking contract committee, which will
influence city decisions. This would be so even if the other body
is solely advisory in nature. (Barisone Advice Letter, No. I-90-
751.)

Our File No. I-92-063
Page 5


from participating in governmental decisions which indirectly have
a material financial effect on the bank.

A multitude of decisions may have an indirect effect on the
bank, from taxing property in the city, including the bank's
property, to imposing fees on all city contractors. Whether the
indirect effect of a decision is material depends on the financial
size of the business entity. Regulation 18702.2 provides
different thresholds of materiality for the following:

     1. Business entities listed on the New York Stock Exchange
or the American Stock Exchange, or business entities on the
Fortune Magazine Directory of the 500 largest U.S. industrial
corporations or the 500 largest U.S. nonindustrial corporations.
(Regulation 18702.2(a) and (d).)

     2. Business entities listed on the National Association of
Securities Dealers National Market List or any business entity
with net tangible assets of at least $18,000,000 and pre-tax
income for the last fiscal year of at least $2,500,000.
(Regulation 18702.2(b) and (e).)

     3. Business entities not fitting the requirements of (a) or
(b) but which are listed on the Pacific Stock Exchange or qualify
for public sale in California and are listed on eligible
securities list maintained by the California Department of
Corporations. Or, business entities with net tangible assets of
at least $4,000,000 and had pre-tax income for the last fiscal
year of at least $750,000, with net income from the period of at
least $400,000. (Regulation 18702.2(c) and (f).)

     4. For any business entity not covered in one of the
categories set forth above, Regulation 18702.2(g) governs.

     You have not specified any decisions concerning potential
indirect effects on the Bank of America, nor do we have financial
information pertaining to Bank of America. Thus, we must leave
the factual determination of materiality to you and Ms. Band
within the parameters of Regulation 18702.2. If questions should
arise concerning indirect effects, Ms. Band should contact us for
further advice.5


5     Under some circumstances a disqualified official may
participate in a decisions if the effect on the official's
interest is not distinguishable from the effect on the public
generally. For the "public generally" exception to apply, the
decision must affect Ms. Band's interests in substantially the
same manner as it will affect a significant segment of the
jurisdiction. (Regulation 18703; In re Owen (1976) 2 FPPC Ops.
77.) Of course, this analysis is necessarily dependent on the
facts pertaining to a specific decision.

Our File No. I-92-063
Page 6


I trust this letter has addressed your concerns.  Should you
have any further questions regarding this matter please feel free
to contact me at (916) 322-5901.6

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel

By: John W. Wallace
Counsel, Legal Division

SH:JWW:aa




5 Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries.  Alternatively, copies of these materials and
commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of 10¢ per page.

sections.question:
As an investment analyst with the City of Santa Rosa, what restrictions does the Act impose on Ms. Band were she to accept employment with Bank of America which has numerous dealings with the city?

¹, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

This committee will also evaluate the bids, if bids are taken, and make recommendations to the city.

Ms. Band has recently been offered a part-time position at the Bank of America. You are concerned about potential conflicts of interest that may result.
sections.analysis:
Public Officials

Section 32048 defines "public official" as every member, officer, employee or consultant of a state or local government agency. As an employee of the City of Santa Rosa, Ms. Band is a public official subject to the conflict of interest provisions of the Act.

Section 87100 of the Act prohibits a public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise using her official position to influence a governmental decision in which she has a financial interest.3 You stated in our telephone conversation and in your letter that as investment analyst for the City of Santa Rosa, Ms. Band is responsible for the day-to-day investment of city funds. You also stated that her position was more than purely clerical or ministerial and that her position entailed the making or participating in the making of decisions.

Section 87103 specifies that a public official has a financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from the effect on the public generally, on the official or a member of his or her immediate family or on:

(c) Any source of income, other than gifts and other than loans by a commercial lending institution in the regular course of business on terms available to the public without regard to official status, aggregating two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value provided to, received by or promised to the public official within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is made.

In your letter you stated that Ms. Band wished to accept a salaried position with Bank of America. Any person or business entity, including Bank of America, that makes a payment to Ms. Band is a source of income to her for the purposes of Section 87103. Consequently, if Bank of America has been a source of income to Ms. Band of $250 or more within the 12 months prior to any decision which will reasonably foreseeably and materially financially affect Bank of America, Ms. Band may not participate.

3 The Act also requires that every public official, including designated employees, disclose all their economic interests that could foreseeably be affected by the exercise of the official's duties. (Sections 81002(c), 87200-87313.) Section 82019 defines those employees who must be designated as any employees whose position entails the making or participation in the making of decisions which may foreseeably have a material effect on any financial interest. Since you stated that Ms. Band makes or participates in making decisions which could foreseeably have a material financial effect on her financial interest, she should be designated in the city's conflict of interest code and is subject to the disclosure provisions of the Act.

in the decision.4

Foreseeability and Materiality

Whether the financial consequences of a decision are reasonably foreseeable at the time a governmental decision is made depends on the facts of each particular case. An effect is considered reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial likelihood that it will occur. Certainty is not required. However, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is not reasonably foreseeable. (In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.)

However, in addition, the foreseeable effect on Ms. Band’s source of income must also be material to require disqualification. The Commission has adopted differing guidelines to determine whether an effect is material, depending on the specific circumstances of each decision.

A. Interests Directly Involved in a Decision.

Where a source of income is directly before the city, as an applicant or the subject of the decision, Regulation 18702.1(a) provides that the effect of the decision on a source of income is deemed material and disqualification is required. (Combs Advice Letter, No. A-89-177.) A source of income is directly before the city when the source of income: (1) initiates the proceeding by filing an application, claim, appeal, or similar request; (2) is a named party in the proceeding; or (3) is the subject of a proceeding because the decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the subject person or business entity. (Regulation 18702.1(b).)

Thus, where decisions involve contracts with the Bank of America, investments in the bank, or new contracts on which the bank has or will bid, the bank would be directly involved in the decision and Ms. Band would not be able to participate.

B. Interests Indirectly Involved in a Decision.

Even if Bank of America is not directly involved in an agency decision, Ms. Band will still be required to disqualify herself

4 Please note, if Ms. Band has a conflict of interest as an investment advisor, she will also have the same conflict of interest with respect to the decisions of other governmental entities, such as the banking contract committee, which will influence city decisions. This would be so even if the other body is solely advisory in nature. (Barisone Advice Letter, No. I-90-751.)

from participating in governmental decisions which indirectly have a material financial effect on the bank.

A multitude of decisions may have an indirect effect on the bank, from taxing property in the city, including the bank's property, to imposing fees on all city contractors. Whether the indirect effect of a decision is material depends on the financial size of the business entity. Regulation 18702.2 provides different thresholds of materiality for the following:

1. Business entities listed on the New York Stock Exchange or the American Stock Exchange, or business entities on the Fortune Magazine Directory of the 500 largest U.S. industrial corporations or the 500 largest U.S. nonindustrial corporations. (Regulation 18702.2(a) and (d).)

2. Business entities listed on the National Association of Securities Dealers National Market List or any business entity with net tangible assets of at least $18,000,000 and pre-tax income for the last fiscal year of at least $2,500,000. (Regulation 18702.2(b) and (e).)

3. Business entities not fitting the requirements of (a) or (b) but which are listed on the Pacific Stock Exchange or qualify for public sale in California and are listed on the Eligible Securities List maintained by the California Department of Corporations. Or, business entities with net tangible assets of at least $4,000,000, and had pre-tax income for the last fiscal year of at least $750,000, with net income from that period of at least $400,000. (Regulation 18702.2(c) and (f).)

4. For any business entity not covered in one of the categories set forth above, Regulation 18702.2(q) governs.

You have not specified any decisions concerning potential indirect effects on the Bank of America, nor do we have financial information pertaining to Bank of America. Thus, we must leave the factual determination of materiality to you and Ms. Band within the parameters of Regulation 18702.2. If questions should arise concerning indirect effects, Ms. Band should contact us for further advice.5

5 Under some circumstances a disqualified official may participate in a decisions if the effect on the official's interest is not distinguishable from the effect on the public generally. For the "public generally" exception to apply, the decision must affect Ms. Band's interests in substantially the same manner as it will affect a significant segment of the jurisdiction. (Regulation 18703; In re Owen (1976) 2 FPPC Ops. 77.) Of course, this analysis is necessarily dependent on the facts pertaining to a specific decision.

I trust this letter has addressed your concerns. Should you have any further questions regarding this matter please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5901.6

QUESTION: As an investment analyst with the City of Santa Rosa, what restrictions does the Act impose on Ms. Band were she to accept employment with Bank of America which has numerous dealings with the city?

¹, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

CONCLUSION: As a public official employed by the City of Santa Rosa, Ms. Band will be prohibited from making, participating in making or in any way attempting to use her official position to influence a governmental decision which will have a foreseeable and material financial effect, distinguishable from the effect on the public generally, on any source of income of $250 or more within 12 months prior to the decision.
